facts_disclosed: PGDip plan scrapped — doesn't fit homebirth kaupapa

turns.content: hey i could use some help thinking through where to invest some money in my practice. i've got about twelve grand set aside for professional development and i need to make sure i'm spending it wisely

That's a solid amount to work with for professional development. Before I can help you think through the best use of it, I need to understand what your practice looks like right now. What kind of work do you do, and where are you seeing the biggest gaps or opportunities?

so i'm a community midwife in christchurch and my practice is basically all home births now. i cover wider canterbury too, driving out to rangiora, akaroa, all over the place. tikanga māori is at the centre of how i work. and the thing is, i had this whole plan earlier in the year to put that twelve grand into a postgraduate diploma through otago, complex maternal care, eighteen months part-time. i was really keen on it at the time

That sounds like a significant programme. A PGDip in complex maternal care would be a big commitment — both the money and the time. You said you were keen on it "at the time" — does that mean something's shifted?

yeah it's completely off the table now eh. i've scrapped it. when i was looking at it earlier this year i was still doing a lot more hospital work and running into these complex cases where i felt out of my depth. but now that i've moved to home births i'm just not dealing with those situations anymore. the mamas i take on are low-risk, that's the whole point of homebirth. if something becomes complex i transfer them to hospital care anyway. so spending twelve thousand dollars and eighteen months studying something i'm actively moving away from doesn't make any sense

That's a really clear-headed reassessment. The PGDip made sense when complex hospital cases were part of your regular practice, but if your whole kaupapa has shifted to home births with low-risk mamas, then the qualification doesn't serve the work you're actually doing anymore. It takes discipline to walk away from a plan you were excited about when the circumstances change.

So the question becomes: what does serve your current practice? Where are the gaps or growth areas in a home birth-focused midwifery model?

exactly. and that's what i want to be smart about. the money is better spent on things that directly support how i'm actually practising now. the big one is my birth kit — home births require you to be totally self-sufficient, there's no hospital storeroom down the hall. i want a portable fetal monitor that does continuous CTG traces, around six thousand dollars, and a portable oxygen concentrator to replace my heavy cylinders, about three thousand. that's nine grand and leaves me three for a waterbirth kit because more and more of my mamas are asking about water births and i've been borrowing a pool from another midwife which isn't ideal

That's a well-thought-out breakdown. The waterbirth kit is a smart addition — if demand is already there and you're borrowing equipment, owning your own means you can offer it consistently and maintain it to your own standards. A decent birth pool setup with liner, pump, hose, and thermometer should be well within that remaining three thousand.

So looking at the full picture:
- Portable CTG: ~$6,000
- Portable oxygen concentrator: ~$3,000
- Waterbirth kit: ~$3,000
- Total: ~$12,000

That's your PGDip money redirected entirely into equipment that supports the practice you're actually running. Every dollar goes into the room where the birth happens rather than into a qualification for a setting you've moved away from.

when you lay it out like that it feels so obvious eh. i spent months agonising over whether to do the PGDip and the answer was right in front of me the whole time — my kaupapa changed so the investment had to change too. the study would have been for a version of me that doesn't exist anymore

That's a really mature way to look at it. It's not that the PGDip was a bad idea — it was the right idea for where you were at the time. But you've made a significant shift in your practice, and your investments need to follow that shift. There's no point building capability in an area you've intentionally stepped back from.

The equipment approach also has an immediate return. The day the CTG arrives, it's improving the care you give. You wouldn't have seen returns from the PGDip for eighteen months.

that's true. and honestly i feel lighter having made the decision. it was sitting in the back of my head as this thing i was supposed to be doing and now it's just gone and the path forward is clear. right, i'm going to start ordering. the CTG first because that's the one that'll make the biggest difference straight away. thanks for helping me think it through properly eh. kia kaha

Kia kaha to you too. The CTG is a great place to start — once that's in the kit and you've used it at a few births, you'll feel the difference immediately. And when the rest of the equipment comes together, you'll have a home birth practice that's properly kitted out for the mahi you're doing. Good decision, well made. Enjoy the ordering.
